Python & Data Science Environment Setup¶

Today we will set up your data science workbench.

This includes:

  • Installing the Python interpreter
  • Learning essential terminal commands
  • Setting up Git and GitHub Desktop
  • Creating and using virtual environments
  • Installing core data science libraries
  • Launching and using JupyterLab

What is the Python Interpreter?¶

A Python interpreter is the program that:

  • Reads your Python code
  • Executes it line by line
  • Produces output

Download Python¶

Get Python 3 from:

https://www.python.org/downloads/

Installation Notes¶

  • Windows: Check “Add Python to PATH”
  • Mac: Use the official installer

What is a Terminal?

  • Run programs
  • Navigate folders
  • Install packages
  • Use Git

Why Use a Terminal?¶

You will use it to:

Manage Files¶

  • Navigate folders
  • Create directories

Manage Software¶

  • Install packages

Run Tools¶

  • Python
  • JupyterLab

Opening the Terminal

Windows

  1. Open the Start Menu
  2. Type PowerShell
  3. Click Windows PowerShell

Mac

  1. Press ⌘ + Space
  2. Type Terminal
  3. Press Enter

Checking Python Installation

Run One Of:

python --version
python3 --version
py --version  (Windows)

Expected Output

Python 3.12.x

Python Version in Terminal¶

Basic Terminal Commands¶

Here are the most important commands you’ll use:

Command What It Does
ls Lists files in the current directory
cd folder_name Changes into a directory named folder_name
cd .. Moves up one directory
mkdir folder_name Creates a new directory named folder_name
pwd Prints the current working directory
clear Clears the console
rm Removes a file from the directory

Example Use of Terminal¶

What is Git?¶

Git is a version control system. It allows you to:

  • Track changes in code
  • Save snapshots of your work
  • Revert to earlier versions
  • Collaborate with others

Creating a GitHub Account¶

GitHub is a website that stores Git repositories online.

In this course, we will use GitHub to:

  • Store your projects in the cloud
  • Submit assignments
  • Track changes to your work
  • Share code

Go to GitHub¶

Visit https://github.com/.

Click Sign up in the top-right corner.

GitHub Account Creation¶

You will be asked to provide:

  • An email address
  • A password
  • A username

Choose a professional username (you may use GitHub in future internships or jobs).

After signing up, GitHub will send you a verification email. Open it and confirm your account.

Log In¶

Once your account is verified, log in at:

https://github.com/login

You now have a GitHub account.

Next, we will connect GitHub to GitHub Desktop so you can easily clone repositories, commit changes, and push your work.

Forking a Repository¶

To submit your own work, you need your own copy of the course repository.

We do this using something called a fork.

What is a Fork?¶

A fork creates a copy of a repository under your own GitHub account.

After you fork:

  • You can make changes freely
  • You can push updates
  • You control your version
  • The original course repository remains unchanged

Why Fork?¶

If you clone the instructor's repository directly, you cannot push changes to it.

By forking:

  • You create your own version
  • You push to your version
  • You submit the link to your repository

GitHub Dekstop¶

We will use GitHub Desktop for simplicity. Download it here:

https://desktop.github.com/

Cloning a Repository¶

Cloning means downloading a repository from GitHub to your computer. After copying and pasting the the link to the repository, open up GitHub Desktop, click on File, and choose the clone repository option.

Getting Repo URL¶

Click on the code button and then copy the URL for the repository.

Cloning in GitHub Desktop¶

Paste the URL for the repository into the box provided in GitHub Desktop. Select a local folder and click clone.

Cloning to Local Folder¶

You can now navigate to this repository in the terminal on your machine.

Virtual Environments¶

A virtual environment is an isolated Python environment. These are useful because different projects may require:

  • Different package versions
  • Different dependencies

Virtual environments prevent conflicts between projects.

Creating a Virtual Environment¶

A virtual environment keeps your project’s Python packages isolated from other projects. Open your terminal and move into your project directory:

cd my_project

This is the folder where we want to create the virtual environment.

Create the Virtual Environment¶

Run:

python -m venv venv

This creates a new folder named venv inside your project.

Activate the Virtual Environment¶

On Mac / Linux¶

source venv/bin/activate

On Windows (PowerShell)¶

venv\Scripts\activate

Confirm It Worked¶

You should now see (venv) at the beginning of your terminal line, for example:

(venv) user@computer my_project %

This means your virtual environment is active. Don't do this right now, but when you're finished working, in the terminal run:

deactivate

Install Data Science Libraries¶

Once your virtual environment is activated, install the required libraries by running:

pip install numpy pandas matplotlib seaborn scikit-learn jupyterlab

This command installs most required packages for the course in one step.

Library Documentation¶

Library Purpose Documentation
NumPy Numerical computing and arrays https://numpy.org/doc/
Pandas Data manipulation and DataFrames https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/
Matplotlib Core plotting library https://matplotlib.org/stable/contents.html
Seaborn Statistical visualization https://seaborn.pydata.org/
Scikit-Learn Machine learning tools https://scikit-learn.org/stable/
JupyterLab Interactive notebook environment https://jupyterlab.readthedocs.io/

Running JupyterLab¶

Now we are ready to run JupyterLab. In the terminal, run:

jupyter lab

This will start a local server on your machine where JupyterLab is running.

JupyterLab Interface Overview¶

File Browser and Launcher (Left Panel)¶

Browse and open files. Create notebooks, consoles, or terminals.

Notebook Area¶

Write and execute code.

Terminal (Inside JupyterLab)¶

You can open a terminal inside JupyterLab if needed.
